name: hcl
context: HashiCorp Configuration Language — the declarative syntax for writing Terraform infrastructure-as-code configs
info: HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language) is the primary language for writing Terraform configurations. It uses a block-based, declarative syntax designed to describe infrastructure resources, data sources, variables, outputs, and providers. HCL files use the .tf extension and are parsed together within a module directory. The language supports arguments (key = value), blocks (labeled containers), expressions, comments (# and /* */), and interpolation. HCL is purpose-built for infrastructure definition — more readable than JSON, more structured than YAML. Terraform also accepts JSON-based .tf.json files as an alternative syntax. See [[terraform/hcl/variables]] for input/output data flow, [[terraform/hcl/expressions]] for computation and logic, and [[terraform/hcl/functions]] for built-in callable functions.
triggers: how does terraform file structure work
body:
# HCL — HashiCorp Configuration Language

HCL is the declarative language used to write Terraform configurations. Every `.tf` file in a directory is parsed as part of the same module. HCL uses a block-based syntax that is both human-readable and machine-friendly.

## Basic Syntax

HCL consists of **arguments** and **blocks**:

```hcl
# Arguments assign a value to a name
ami = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"

# Blocks are containers with a type, optional labels, and a body
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t2.micro"
  tags = {
    Name = "web-server"
  }
}
```

Blocks have a **type** (e.g. `resource`), zero or more **labels** (e.g. `"aws_instance"` `"web"`), and a body enclosed in `{ }`. Arguments inside blocks use `=` to assign values.

## Block Types

Terraform defines these top-level block types:

```hcl
# terraform — settings, required providers, backend config
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.5.0"
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
  backend "s3" {
    bucket = "my-tf-state"
    key    = "prod/terraform.tfstate"
    region = "us-east-1"
  }
}

# provider — configure a provider (AWS, GCP, Azure, etc.)
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
}

# resource — declare an infrastructure object
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t2.micro"
  tags = {
    Name = "web-server"
  }
}

# data — read from an existing resource or external source
data "aws_ami" "ubuntu" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["099720109477"]
  filter {
    name   = "name"
    values = ["ubuntu/images/hvm-ssd/ubuntu-jammy-22.04-amd64-server-*"]
  }
}

# variable — declare an input variable
variable "instance_type" {
  type        = string
  default     = "t2.micro"
  description = "EC2 instance type"
}

# output — expose a value from the module
output "instance_ip" {
  value       = aws_instance.web.public_ip
  description = "Public IP of the web server"
}

# locals — define computed local values
locals {
  common_tags = {
    Environment = var.environment
    Project     = "my-app"
    ManagedBy   = "terraform"
  }
}

# module — call a child module
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "5.1.0"
  cidr    = "10.0.0.0/16"
}
```

## Comments

HCL supports three comment styles:

```hcl
# Single-line comment (most common)
// Single-line comment (alternative)
/* Multi-line
   comment block */
```

## File Structure

A typical Terraform project organizes files by purpose:

```
project/
  main.tf          # Primary resource definitions
  variables.tf     # Input variable declarations
  outputs.tf       # Output value declarations
  providers.tf     # Provider configuration
  terraform.tf     # Terraform settings and backend
  terraform.tfvars # Variable values (not committed for secrets)
  modules/         # Local reusable modules
    vpc/
      main.tf
      variables.tf
      outputs.tf
```

All `.tf` files in a directory are merged — file names are for human organization, not Terraform parsing. The `.tfvars` files provide variable values and are loaded automatically when named `terraform.tfvars` or `*.auto.tfvars`.

## Value Types

HCL supports these primitive and complex types:

```hcl
# Primitive types
name    = "web-server"      # string
count   = 3                  # number
enabled = true               # bool

# Complex types
tags    = { Name = "web" }   # map/object
subnets = ["subnet-a", "subnet-b"]  # list/tuple
cidrs   = toset(["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24"])  # set
```

See [[terraform/hcl/variables]] for type constraints and [[terraform/hcl/expressions]] for how to reference and compute values.

sections.title: Basic syntax — arguments and blocks
sections.body: HCL files contain arguments (`key = value`) and blocks (`type "label" { ... }`). Blocks have a type keyword, zero or more string labels, and a body in braces. Arguments inside blocks assign values to that block's attributes. Nesting blocks inside blocks is supported for sub-configurations (e.g. `filter` inside `data`, `backend` inside `terraform`).

sections.title: Terraform block types
sections.body: The eight top-level block types are: `terraform` (settings, required_providers, backend), `provider` (provider configuration), `resource` (infrastructure objects to create/manage), `data` (read-only data sources), `variable` (input parameters — see [[terraform/hcl/variables]]), `output` (exported values), `locals` (computed helper values), and `module` (calls to reusable child modules). Each block type has specific label requirements — `resource` and `data` take two labels (type and name), `variable` and `output` take one.

sections.title: Comments
sections.body: Three comment syntaxes: `#` for single-line (idiomatic), `//` for single-line (alternative), and `/* ... */` for multi-line blocks. The `#` style is preferred by `terraform fmt`.

sections.title: File structure and organization
sections.body: All `.tf` files in a directory form one module. Common convention: `main.tf` for resources, `variables.tf` for inputs, `outputs.tf` for exports, `providers.tf` for provider config, `terraform.tf` for settings/backend. File names are cosmetic — Terraform merges them all. Variable values go in `terraform.tfvars` or `*.auto.tfvars`. The `.terraform/` directory (created by `terraform init`) holds provider plugins and module cache — add it to `.gitignore`.

sections.title: Value types
sections.body: Primitives: `string`, `number`, `bool`. Collections: `list(type)`, `set(type)`, `map(type)`. Structural: `object({...})`, `tuple([...])`. The `any` keyword accepts any type. Use type constraints in variable blocks to enforce expected shapes. See [[terraform/hcl/variables]] for details on type declarations and [[terraform/hcl/functions]] for type conversion functions like `tostring`, `tolist`, `tomap`.
